
```markdown
---
title: "The European Football Divide"
subtitle: "Comparing competitive inequality across Europe's top 5 leagues (1992-2025)"
author: "Eric Schnitger"
date: 2026-04-27
categories: [data-visualization, python, sports-analytics, web-scraping, comparative]
image: thumbnail.png
description: |
 A cross-league comparison of how the gap between title winners and
 relegation survivors has evolved across the Premier League, Bundesliga,
 La Liga, Ligue 1, and Serie A over 33 years.
format:
 html:
   code-fold: true
   code-summary: "Show code"
   code-tools: true
   toc: true
   toc-depth: 3
execute:
 echo: true
 warning: false
 freeze: auto
---

## The Question

Every top European league has a dominant team or small elite. But are the
dynamics the same everywhere? Is the Premier League's growing gap unique, or
is stratification a continent-wide phenomenon?

## Key Findings

::: {.callout-tip}
## Summary

- **All five leagues show a widening gap** since 1992, but at different rates
- **The Bundesliga has the highest average gap** (when normalized for season length), because Bayern's dominance was already entrenched
- **Ligue 1** shows the most dramatic single inflection point (PSG takeover, 2011)
- **The Premier League and La Liga** have the steepest *rate of increase* in the gap
- **Serie A** is the most volatile due to Calciopoli and financial instability
- **No league has reversed the trend** through regulation alone
:::

## Individual League Reports

::: {.grid style="--bs-columns: 3; gap: 1.5rem;"}

::: {.g-col-1}
### [Premier League](premier-league/)
The gap has nearly doubled since 1992.
:::

::: {.g-col-1}
### [Bundesliga](bundesliga/)
Bayern's grip predates the modern era.
:::

::: {.g-col-1}
### [La Liga](la-liga/)
Barca and Real pushed each other to extremes.
:::

::: {.g-col-1}
### [Ligue 1](ligue-1/)
PSG transformed it overnight.
:::

::: {.g-col-1}
### [Serie A](serie-a/)
From seven contenders to one dynasty.
:::

:::

---

## Data Loading

```{python}
#| label: load-all
#| code-summary: "Load all five leagues"

import sys
sys.path.insert(0, ".")

from _utils import (
   LEAGUES, scrape_league, enrich_dataframe,
   build_comparison_chart, build_frequency_tables,
)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Load all leagues
league_data = {}
for key in ["premier_league", "bundesliga", "la_liga", "ligue_1", "serie_a"]:
   config = LEAGUES[key]
   cache_dir = f"{key.replace('_', '-')}/"  # e.g., "premier-league/"
   raw = scrape_league(key, cache_dir=cache_dir)
   league_data[key] = enrich_dataframe(raw)
   print(f"  {config.name}: {len(league_data[key])} seasons")

print(f"\nTotal: {sum(len(v) for v in league_data.values())} season-records across 5 leagues")
```

## The Gap Over Time (Normalized)

Since the Bundesliga and Ligue 1 play 34 matches per season (vs. 38 in the
other leagues), raw point totals aren't directly comparable. The chart below
normalizes everything to a **38-game equivalent** so the gaps are on equal
footing.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gap-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Points gap (normalized to 38-game season) across all five leagues"
#| code-summary: "Normalized gap comparison"

fig = build_comparison_chart(
   league_data,
   metric="Gap (38-game)",
   title="The Growing Divide: Gap Between Champions and Survival (Normalized)",
)
fig.show()
```

## Title-Winning Points (Normalized)

```{python}
#| label: fig-title-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Title-winning points (38-game equivalent) across all five leagues"
#| code-summary: "Title points comparison"

fig = build_comparison_chart(
   league_data,
   metric="Title Pts (38-game)",
   title="Title-Winning Points Across Europe (38-Game Equivalent)",
)
fig.show()
```

## Relegation Survival Points (Normalized)

```{python}
#| label: fig-survival-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Survival points (38-game equivalent) across all five leagues"
#| code-summary: "Survival points comparison"

fig = build_comparison_chart(
   league_data,
   metric="Survival Pts (38-game)",
   title="Relegation Survival Points Across Europe (38-Game Equivalent)",
)
fig.show()
```

## Points Ratio: Who Has the Most Unequal League?

The ratio of champion points to survival points is unit-free and directly
comparable across leagues regardless of season length.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ratio-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Points ratio (champion / survival) across all five leagues"
#| code-summary: "Ratio comparison"

fig = build_comparison_chart(
   league_data,
   metric="Ratio",
   title="Competitive Inequality: Champion-to-Survival Points Ratio",
)
fig.update_layout(yaxis=dict(title="Ratio (champion pts / survival pts)"))
fig.show()
```

## League Summary Statistics

```{python}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Key statistics by league (1992-2025, normalized to 38 games)"
#| code-summary: "Summary statistics table"

summary_rows = []
for key, df in league_data.items():
   config = LEAGUES[key]
   summary_rows.append({
       "League": config.name,
       "Country": config.country,
       "Seasons": len(df),
       "Teams": config.num_teams,
       "Avg Title Pts (38g)": round(df["Title Pts (38-game)"].mean(), 1)
           if "Title Pts (38-game)" in df.columns else None,
       "Avg Survival Pts (38g)": round(df["Survival Pts (38-game)"].mean(), 1)
           if "Survival Pts (38-game)" in df.columns else None,
       "Avg Gap (38g)": round(df["Gap (38-game)"].mean(), 1)
           if "Gap (38-game)" in df.columns else None,
       "Avg Ratio": round(df["Ratio"].mean(), 2),
       "Max Ratio": round(df["Ratio"].max(), 2),
       "Gap Trend (pts/yr)": None,  # Calculated below
   })
   # Calculate gap trend slope
   if "Gap (38-game)" in df.columns:
       x_num = np.arange(len(df))
       valid = df["Gap (38-game)"].notna()
       if valid.sum() >= 3:
           slope = np.polyfit(x_num[valid], df.loc[valid, "Gap (38-game)"].values, 1)[0]
           summary_rows[-1]["Gap Trend (pts/yr)"] = round(slope, 3)

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(summary_rows)
summary_df.style.format({
   "Avg Ratio": "{:.2f}x",
   "Max Ratio": "{:.2f}x",
   "Gap Trend (pts/yr)": "{:+.3f}",
}).hide(axis="index")
```

## Champions League Concentration: Cross-League Comparison

How concentrated is the top 4 across each league?

```{python}
#| label: fig-concentration
#| fig-cap: "Competitive concentration: what percentage of Top 4 slots are held by the top 3 clubs in each league"
#| code-summary: "Concentration comparison"

concentration_data = []
for key, df in league_data.items():
   config = LEAGUES[key]
   top4_clubs, _ = build_frequency_tables(df, config)
   total_slots = len(df) * 4
   top3_share = top4_clubs.head(3)["Top 4 Finishes"].sum() / total_slots * 100
   concentration_data.append({
       "League": config.name,
       "Top 3 Clubs Share (%)": round(top3_share, 1),
       "Color": config.color,
   })

conc_df = pd.DataFrame(concentration_data).sort_values(
   "Top 3 Clubs Share (%)", ascending=True
)

fig_conc = go.Figure(data=[go.Bar(
   x=conc_df["Top 3 Clubs Share (%)"],
   y=conc_df["League"],
   orientation="h",
   marker=dict(color=conc_df["Color"].tolist()),
   hovertemplate="<b>%{y}</b><br>Top 3 clubs hold %{x:.1f}% of all Top 4 slots<extra></extra>",
)])

fig_conc.update_layout(
   template="plotly_dark",
   title=dict(
       text="Competitive Concentration: Top 3 Clubs' Share of All Top-4 Finishes",
       font=dict(size=16),
   ),
   xaxis=dict(title="Percentage of Top 4 Slots (%)", range=[0, 100]),
   yaxis=dict(title=""),
   height=400,
   width=900,
)

fig_conc.show()
```

## Decade-by-Decade Gap Evolution

```{python}
#| label: fig-decades
#| fig-cap: "Average gap by decade across all leagues (38-game normalized)"
#| code-summary: "Decade comparison"

decade_data = []
for key, df in league_data.items():
   config = LEAGUES[key]
   if "Gap (38-game)" not in df.columns or "Start Year" not in df.columns:
       continue
   df_copy = df.copy()
   df_copy["Decade"] = (df_copy["Start Year"] // 10) * 10
   df_copy["Decade Label"] = df_copy["Decade"].apply(lambda d: f"{d}s")
   for decade, group in df_copy.groupby("Decade Label"):
       decade_data.append({
           "League": config.name,
           "Decade": decade,
           "Avg Gap (38-game)": round(group["Gap (38-game)"].mean(), 1),
           "Color": config.color,
       })

decade_df = pd.DataFrame(decade_data)

fig_decade = go.Figure()
for key in league_data:
   config = LEAGUES[key]
   league_decades = decade_df[decade_df["League"] == config.name].sort_values("Decade")
   fig_decade.add_trace(go.Bar(
       x=league_decades["Decade"],
       y=league_decades["Avg Gap (38-game)"],
       name=config.name,
       marker=dict(color=config.color, opacity=0.85),
       hovertemplate=f"<b>{config.name}</b><br>%{{x}}<br>Avg Gap: %{{y:.1f}} pts<extra></extra>",
   ))

fig_decade.update_layout(
   template="plotly_dark",
   title=dict(text="Average Gap by Decade (38-Game Normalized)", font=dict(size=16)),
   barmode="group",
   xaxis=dict(title="Decade"),
   yaxis=dict(title="Avg Points Gap (38-game equivalent)"),
   height=500,
   width=1000,
   legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
)

fig_decade.show()
```

## The Story

### 1990s: Relative Parity

In the early 1990s, all five leagues had relatively modest gaps between top
and bottom. Title-winning totals were lower (70-85 points), and survival
required more points (35-50). The financial playing field, while never truly
level, was less extreme.

### 2000s: The Money Arrives

The Champions League's expanding revenue model, combined with individual TV
deals (La Liga) and oligarch ownership (Chelsea, Manchester City), began
widening the gap. Title-winning points climbed while survival stayed flat.

### 2010s: The Acceleration

This decade saw the most dramatic changes:

- **PSG's takeover** (2011) created instant and massive inequality in France
- **Juventus's nine titles** (2012-2020) locked out all competition in Italy
- **City's 100-point season** (2018) and Liverpool's 97-point response reset expectations in England
- **Messi and Ronaldo** pushed La Liga's top two to unprecedented heights

### 2020s: A Slight Correction?

The post-COVID era has seen marginally lower title-winning points in some
leagues, but the structural gap remains. The top clubs' financial advantages
(Champions League revenue, global commercial deals, wage-spending capacity)
continue to compound year over year.

## Conclusions

1. **The divergence is universal.** Every top-5 European league is more
  stratified in 2025 than it was in 1992.

2. **The causes are structural.** TV revenue concentration, Champions League
  money flowing to the same clubs, and the compounding effect of success
  (better players lead to more revenue lead to better players) create a
  feedback loop that regulation has failed to break.

3. **The rate differs by league.** The Premier League and La Liga show the
  steepest acceleration. The Bundesliga was already unequal. Ligue 1 had a
  single shock event (PSG). Serie A is the most chaotic.

4. **Survival requirements are universal.** Regardless of league, ~33-37 points
  (38-game equivalent) is the survival threshold. The bottom of the table is
  constrained by the same dynamics everywhere.

5. **No regulation has worked.** Neither the 50+1 rule (Germany), collective TV
  deals (Spain post-2016), Financial Fair Play (Europe-wide), nor the Profit
  and Sustainability rules (England) have reversed the trend.

## Methodology

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Technical details

**Data source**: All data scraped from Wikipedia's season-by-season articles
using `requests` and `pandas.read_html()`.

**Normalization**: Since leagues have different numbers of teams (18 or 20) and
therefore different match counts (34 or 38), raw points are not directly
comparable. All cross-league comparisons use points-per-game (PPG) multiplied by
38 to produce a "38-game equivalent" that puts all leagues on equal footing.

**Relegation definitions**: To enable fair comparison, "relegation zone" is
defined as the bottom 3 teams in all leagues, regardless of whether the 3rd
plays a playoff (as in the Bundesliga and Ligue 1). "Survived relegation"
is always the team immediately above the relegation zone.

**Caching**: Each league's data is cached to a local CSV with 7-day expiry.
The `freeze: auto` Quarto directive further ensures notebooks only re-execute
when source code changes.

**Code reuse**: All five league pages and this comparison page share a common
utility module (`_utils.py`) for scraping, enrichment, and charting, ensuring
consistent methodology across the analysis.
:::

## Source Code

The complete source for this project is on
[GitHub](https://github.com/yourusername/portfolio/tree/main/projects/european-football-divide).

---

*Built with Python, Pandas, NumPy, Plotly, and Requests. Published with Quarto.*
```
